content:
# Agent Teams Phase 4: LLM Extraction and Scope Promotion -- Decision Record (2026-03-29)
## What: Added optional LLM extraction pass that identifies decisions, findings, and questions in team message entries and promotes product-relevant ones to the corvia scope.
## Key Design Decisions:
1. Two-component architecture: Adapter writes source_versions to .classify-queue (per-team, crash-safe), server handles LLM extraction and promotion via POST /v1/classify/agent-teams. Adapter stays simple (no HTTP/gRPC deps), server has access to GenerationEngine and GraphStore.
2. Two-stage LLM pipeline: First call extracts structured items (JSON array of decision/finding/question), second call classifies each for product relevance (YES/NO). Extraction always persists to user-history scope regardless of promotion outcome, preserving full provenance.
3. Source_version uses parent entry_ref as prefix (e.g. team:messages:task-1:extracted:1) to avoid cross-batch collisions. The global counter approach was flagged by all 5 reviewers during the review phase.
4. Embedding reuse for promoted entries: promoted copy reuses the extracted entry's embedding vector instead of re-computing it (content is identical). Saves one inference call per promotion.
5. Response separates rejected (entry not found) from skipped (nothing to extract) from failed (transient errors) for clear operational metrics.
## Review Findings Worth Remembering:
- MockGenerationEngine returns the same response for all calls. To test both extraction AND promotion, use a response that starts with "YES" and contains valid JSON (e.g. "YES. [{...}]") so the parser finds the array and the promotion classifier sees "YES".
- Per-team .classify-queue writes match the crash-safety pattern established for .ingested in Phase 2. Batching queue writes to end-of-run was flagged as a regression from that pattern.
## What Blocked / Surprised:
- Nothing blocked. RFC Section 7 was well-specified.
- The sv_lookup HashMap borrows from uh_entries, so the cloned entry was unnecessary. Using a reference avoids copying the embedding vector (~3KB per entry).
